Dear Rachel:

This memorandum provides the monthly case status report for the above-referenced matter, covering activity during the reporting period of May 1 through May 31, 2025. Please note that this communication is protected by the attorney-client privilege and constitutes attorney work product prepared in anticipation of litigation. It should not be disclosed to any third party without prior consultation with this firm.

**[I. Executive Summary]{.underline}**

May 2025 was an exceptionally active month in the *Pinnacle Health Systems, Inc. v. MedCore Analytics, LLC* litigation. Three major workstreams dominated the team\'s efforts during the reporting period: (1) receipt and initial review of MedCore\'s third document production, which comprised approximately 85,000 documents and constituted the largest single production in the case to date; (2) the deposition of Dr. Rebecca Liu, one of the three former Pinnacle data scientists at the center of this dispute, which took place on May 19, 2025; and (3) the filing of a motion to compel before Judge Stanton regarding MedCore\'s incomplete and evasive responses to Pinnacle\'s First Set of Interrogatories.

The convergence of these critical workstreams required the litigation team to operate at elevated capacity throughout the month. Mr. Hargrove\'s involvement was significantly more extensive than in prior months due to the critical nature of the Liu deposition, the strategy session with Pinnacle\'s in-house team, and his personal review of the third document production. Specifically, Mr. Hargrove devoted substantial personal attention to the matter this month, including attending the Liu deposition, conducting a comprehensive strategy session with the in-house team, and personally reviewing key documents in the third production. The Liu deposition was staffed to ensure comprehensive coverage given its critical importance to Pinnacle\'s trade secret claims, as Dr. Liu possesses direct knowledge of the proprietary algorithms at the heart of this case.

Looking ahead, the discovery close deadline remains September 30, 2025, leaving the team approximately four months to complete fact depositions, pursue any supplemental discovery compelled by the court, and initiate expert discovery. The sections below provide a detailed account of each workstream and the outlook for June and July.

**[II. Case Background and Current Posture]{.underline}**

For the monthly record, the following summarizes the current posture of the litigation. This action was filed by Pinnacle Health Systems, Inc. against MedCore Analytics, LLC, a Georgia limited liability company headquartered in Alpharetta, Georgia, on September 15, 2024. Pinnacle\'s claims include breach of contract arising from non-disclosure and non-competition agreements, misappropriation of trade secrets under both the Georgia Trade Secrets Act and the federal Defend Trade Secrets Act (\"DTSA\"), 18 U.S.C. § 1836 *et seq.*, and unjust enrichment. The amount in controversy, as stated in Pinnacle\'s complaint, is approximately \$47.5 million in claimed damages.

MedCore, represented by Sternfield Morrison & Katz LLP, has asserted counterclaims against Pinnacle for tortious interference with business relations and seeks a declaratory judgment of non-infringement of any protectable trade secrets.

The core factual allegations center on MedCore\'s hiring of three former Pinnacle data scientists---including Dr. Rebecca Liu---and MedCore\'s alleged use of Pinnacle\'s proprietary patient-outcome prediction algorithms to develop competing analytics products. Pinnacle alleges that the departing employees transported proprietary data and source code to MedCore in violation of their contractual obligations.

Discovery commenced on February 3, 2025, pursuant to the scheduling order entered by Judge Stanton. The discovery close deadline is September 30, 2025. The case is currently in the fact discovery phase, with the team focused on document review, depositions of former Pinnacle employees now employed by MedCore, and interrogatory responses from both sides.

**[III. May 2025 Activity Report]{.underline}**

**[III.A. MedCore\'s Third Document Production]{.underline}**

In early May 2025, MedCore, through its counsel at Sternfield Morrison & Katz LLP, served its third document production in this matter. The production consisted of approximately 85,000 documents totaling over 1.2 million pages, making it by a significant margin the largest single production in the case to date. The prior two productions combined had totaled approximately 40,000 documents. The substantial increase in volume appears attributable to the production of internal MedCore email communications, technical specifications and design documents, source code documentation, and communications involving the three former Pinnacle data scientists, including extensive correspondence to and from Dr. Rebecca Liu regarding algorithm development at MedCore.

The production was hosted and processed by Tripoint Litigation Support LLC, the firm\'s e-discovery hosting vendor. Tripoint completed ingestion, de-duplication, and processing of the production by approximately May 6, at which point the documents were made available for attorney review on the Relativity platform.

Document review was conducted primarily by Marcus Tate, a contract attorney sourced through Redwood Document Solutions, the firm\'s contract attorney staffing vendor. Mr. Tate reviewed documents for relevance, responsiveness, and privilege throughout the month. James Pettersson supervised the document review workflow, established and refined search term protocols, and quality-checked privilege designations on an ongoing basis. On certain days when the volume of documents requiring review exceeded Mr. Tate\'s capacity, Tyler Webb assisted with document review to ensure the team remained on schedule.

David Hargrove personally reviewed a subset of the production documents that appeared most directly relevant to Pinnacle\'s trade secret claims, including technical memoranda authored by the former Pinnacle employees and algorithm-related correspondence between MedCore\'s development team and its product leadership. Mr. Hargrove undertook this review in early May to ensure that the team\'s overall approach to categorization, prioritization, and issue-tagging was properly calibrated to Pinnacle\'s litigation objectives. His direct involvement allowed the team to refine its review protocol and focus resources on the most consequential documents.

The volume of the third production contributed to elevated staffing needs throughout the month. In addition to the core review team, Nina Barros assisted with reviewing deposition transcripts from earlier depositions and conducted research on interrogatory-related issues that arose from the production documents. Priya Sengupta provided additional research and preparation support, particularly with respect to DTSA requirements relevant to categorizing the produced documents. The combined effort of these team members ensured that the review progressed efficiently and that key documents were identified promptly in advance of the Liu deposition and the motion to compel.

**[III.B. Deposition of Dr. Rebecca Liu (May 19, 2025)]{.underline}**

Dr. Rebecca Liu, a former Pinnacle Senior Data Scientist and one of the three employees who departed to join MedCore, was deposed on May 19, 2025. Dr. Liu is a key fact witness in this matter. While employed at Pinnacle, she served as the lead developer of the proprietary patient-outcome prediction algorithms that form the basis of Pinnacle\'s trade secret claims. Pinnacle\'s complaint alleges that Dr. Liu transported proprietary data and source code to MedCore upon her departure and subsequently used that material to develop competing products. Her testimony was therefore anticipated to be among the most consequential in the entire case.

The deposition was taken at the offices of Hargrove & Linden LLP in Atlanta, Georgia. Court reporting services were provided by Grayson Court Reporting Services. Opposing counsel from Sternfield Morrison & Katz LLP represented Dr. Liu at the deposition.

Given Dr. Liu\'s status as the most critical fact witness in this matter and her direct knowledge of the algorithms at issue, Mr. Hargrove determined that his personal attendance was necessary to assess credibility and guide follow-up questioning in real time. Karen Cho conducted the direct examination of Dr. Liu. Mr. Hargrove attended the deposition to observe, provide real-time strategic guidance, and evaluate the witness\'s demeanor and credibility on the key factual issues. James Pettersson also attended to manage the exhibit presentation, take detailed notes, and prepare a comprehensive testimony summary for use in subsequent depositions and potential summary judgment briefing. All three attorneys were present for the full duration of the deposition.

The deposition lasted approximately 6.5 hours of on-the-record testimony and covered substantial ground. Without waiving any privilege, we can report at a general level that Dr. Liu testified regarding her role in developing the patient-outcome algorithms at Pinnacle, the circumstances and reasons for her departure from Pinnacle, the nature of the files and materials she retained or accessed after her departure, and the scope and nature of her work at MedCore. Certain testimony was significant and potentially favorable to Pinnacle\'s trade secret claims, particularly with respect to Dr. Liu\'s acknowledgment of her familiarity with specific proprietary methodologies. Other portions of the testimony raised areas requiring follow-up in subsequent depositions of the other former Pinnacle employees and certain MedCore personnel.

A post-deposition debrief was conducted the same afternoon with the litigation team to assess the testimony, identify areas of strength and concern, and plan follow-up discovery. Karen Cho subsequently conferred with Rachel Dominguez by telephone on May 20 regarding the implications of Dr. Liu\'s testimony for the overall case strategy.

In preparation for the deposition, Mr. Hargrove had participated in a May 8 team strategy conference call to discuss the overall discovery strategy and deposition preparation for the former Pinnacle employee witnesses. That call, which included the full litigation team, addressed the planned line of questioning for Dr. Liu, the prioritization of document review in the third production to support the deposition, and coordination of interrogatory response deadlines. Mr. Hargrove also spent time during the week of May 12 reviewing key documents and outlining strategic themes for the examination. This preparatory work, combined with his attendance at the deposition itself, reflected a level of partner engagement commensurate with the significance of Dr. Liu as a witness.

**[III.C. Motion to Compel --- MedCore\'s Incomplete Interrogatory Responses]{.underline}**

In late May 2025, Hargrove & Linden filed a Motion to Compel on behalf of Pinnacle before Judge Stanton. The motion concerns MedCore\'s incomplete and evasive responses to Pinnacle\'s First Set of Interrogatories (Nos. 1 through 25), which were served on MedCore in March 2025. MedCore\'s responses, received in April, were deficient in several material respects.

The key deficiencies identified in MedCore\'s responses include: (a) the interposition of boilerplate objections---including overbreadth, undue burden, and relevance objections---without providing substantive answers to the underlying questions; (b) failure to identify individuals with knowledge of the algorithm development process at MedCore, despite interrogatories specifically requesting such identification; and (c) incomplete and non-responsive answers regarding MedCore\'s revenue derived from products utilizing the allegedly misappropriated algorithms, which is directly relevant to Pinnacle\'s damages claims.

The motion brief was primarily drafted by Karen Cho. Tyler Webb performed legal research and cite-checking in support of the brief. James Pettersson prepared the exhibits to the motion, including annotated copies of the deficient interrogatory responses, and assisted with drafting certain sections of the brief addressing the factual background. Prior to filing, a meet-and-confer communication was exchanged with Sternfield Morrison & Katz LLP as required under the Local Rules, but opposing counsel was unwilling to supplement the responses.

The motion is currently pending before Judge Stanton and no hearing date has yet been scheduled. Sternfield Morrison & Katz LLP has indicated through correspondence that it intends to oppose the motion.

**[III.D. Interrogatory Responses and Written Discovery]{.underline}**

In parallel with the motion to compel and the deposition, the team continued preparing Pinnacle\'s own responses to MedCore\'s interrogatories and requests for production related to MedCore\'s tortious interference counterclaim and its requests regarding Pinnacle\'s damages methodology. James Pettersson took primary responsibility for drafting these responses, with review and input from Karen Cho to ensure consistency with the overall litigation strategy.

Nina Barros assisted with research on trade secret preemption issues that arose in connection with certain of MedCore\'s interrogatories, which sought information that Pinnacle contends is preempted by its trade secret claims. Priya Sengupta conducted legal research on DTSA requirements relevant to the specificity of trade secret identification in interrogatory responses, an issue that has been the subject of recent case law developments in the Eleventh Circuit.

The team also coordinated with Pinnacle\'s internal IT department and data science team to gather the factual information needed to respond to MedCore\'s technical interrogatories regarding the development history and architecture of the patient-outcome algorithms. This coordination required several telephone calls and email exchanges with Pinnacle personnel during the second and third weeks of May.

**[III.E. Team Strategy and Client Communications]{.underline}**

Two significant strategy sessions took place during May 2025. First, on May 8, the full litigation team participated in a strategy conference call to discuss overall discovery strategy and deposition preparation for the remaining former Pinnacle employee witnesses. The call covered the planned approach for the Liu deposition, the prioritization of document review within the third production to identify documents most relevant to the upcoming depositions, and coordination of deadlines for outstanding interrogatory responses. The call also addressed staffing allocation for the remainder of the discovery period.

Second, on May 27, David Hargrove conducted an in-person strategy session with Pinnacle\'s in-house legal team---Rachel Dominguez and Sandra Whitmore---at Pinnacle\'s offices at 2800 Peachtree Road NE, Suite 1400, Atlanta, Georgia 30305. The session covered several important strategic topics, including the timeline and viability of a potential summary judgment motion following the close of discovery, the retention of expert witnesses for both damages quantification and technical analysis of the algorithm code, and the overall litigation strategy through the September 30, 2025 discovery cutoff. Following the session, Mr. Hargrove drafted an outline for the expert discovery plan, identifying categories of expertise needed, a proposed timeline for retention and report preparation, and preliminary thoughts on potential expert candidates.

In addition to these formal sessions, Karen Cho maintained regular communication with Rachel Dominguez throughout the month regarding case developments, deposition scheduling, and coordination of document collection efforts within Pinnacle.

**[IV. Staffing and Resource Update]{.underline}**

The following team members worked on the *Pinnacle v. MedCore* matter during May 2025:

> • **David Hargrove, Lead Partner** --- Overall case strategy, attendance at the Liu deposition, in-person client strategy session on May 27, personal review of key documents in the third production, and participation in the May 8 team strategy call. Mr. Hargrove\'s involvement was more extensive than in typical months, reflecting the convergence of several critical workstreams---the Liu deposition, the strategy session with Pinnacle\'s in-house team, and his review of key documents in the third production. We anticipate that his hours will return to more typical levels in June and July as the deposition schedule shifts to more junior witnesses and the third-production review approaches completion.
>
> • **Karen Cho, Senior Associate** --- Day-to-day case management, conducted the direct examination at the Liu deposition, drafted the motion to compel, coordinated with opposing counsel at Sternfield Morrison & Katz LLP, and maintained regular communication with Pinnacle\'s in-house team.
>
> • **James Pettersson, Mid-Level Associate** --- Supervised document review workflow and quality-checked privilege designations, drafted Pinnacle\'s interrogatory responses, attended the Liu deposition to manage exhibits and prepare the testimony summary, and assisted with the motion to compel.
>
> • **Nina Barros, Mid-Level Associate** --- Assisted with interrogatory-related research on trade secret preemption issues and reviewed deposition transcripts. Her involvement this month was consistent with the request submitted to Ms. Dominguez on April 28, 2025.
>
> • **Tyler Webb, Junior Associate** --- Legal research and cite-checking for the motion to compel brief, and assisted with document review on days of elevated volume.
>
> • **Priya Sengupta, Junior Associate** --- Legal research on DTSA requirements relevant to interrogatory responses, deposition exhibit binder preparation, and cite-checking.
>
> • **Marcus Tate, Contract Attorney** (via Redwood Document Solutions) --- Primary document reviewer for the third production batch, reviewing for relevance, responsiveness, and privilege.

In total, seven timekeepers billed time to this matter during May 2025. The elevated staffing reflects the volume of work generated by the concurrent workstreams described above. As noted, we expect staffing levels and partner hours to moderate in the coming months as the most intensive phase of the third-production review concludes and the deposition schedule proceeds with witnesses of comparatively lesser complexity.

**[V. Upcoming Work --- June--July 2025 Outlook]{.underline}**

**Depositions.** Two additional depositions of former Pinnacle data scientists---the remaining two of the three employees who departed to join MedCore---are scheduled for June and early July 2025, respectively. These depositions will build on the themes developed in the Liu deposition. Depositions of MedCore corporate personnel are expected to follow in July and August 2025.

**Document Review.** Continued review and analysis of the approximately 85,000-document third production will extend through mid-to-late June, given the volume of material and the need to complete privilege review before the production documents can be fully utilized in depositions and briefing.

**Motion to Compel.** We anticipate a ruling or hearing on the pending motion to compel in June. If the motion is granted in whole or in part, we expect to receive supplemental interrogatory responses from MedCore that may yield significant additional information regarding MedCore\'s use of the algorithms and associated revenue.

**Expert Discovery.** Following the May 27 strategy session, Hargrove & Linden will work with Pinnacle to identify and retain expert witnesses for damages quantification and technical analysis of the proprietary algorithms. Given the September 30 discovery cutoff, expert reports will likely need to be completed by mid-to-late August 2025, which makes the prompt engagement of experts in June a priority.

**MedCore Counterclaim Discovery.** Sternfield Morrison & Katz LLP has indicated that it intends to serve additional discovery requests related to MedCore\'s tortious interference counterclaim. The team will need to prepare Pinnacle\'s responses and coordinate document collection with Pinnacle\'s internal teams.

**General Outlook.** We anticipate that June will be a similarly active month given the remaining deposition schedule and ongoing document review, though we expect total hours to moderate somewhat as the third-production review approaches completion.

We respectfully request your assistance with the following items in the near term:

1\. **Expert Witness Identification.** Please coordinate with Pinnacle\'s data science team to identify potential expert witnesses who may be qualified to provide technical analysis of the patient-outcome algorithms at issue. We would appreciate receiving any recommendations or internal contacts by mid-June so that we can begin the retention process promptly.

2\. **Counterclaim Document Compilation.** In anticipation of MedCore\'s additional discovery requests regarding its tortious interference counterclaim, please begin compiling internal documents and communications related to Pinnacle\'s competitive practices and any interactions with MedCore\'s clients or prospective clients. Early identification of responsive materials will allow us to prepare timely and thorough responses.

{"command": "cd $WORKSPACE_DIR && python3 -c "
import openpyxl
wb = openpyxl.load_workbook('documents/hl-may-2025-invoice.xlsx', data_only=True)
ws = wb['Time Detail']
from collections import defaultdict
totals = defaultdict(lambda: [0.0,0.0])
entries=[]
for r in range(2, ws.max_row+1):
    entry = ws.cell(r,1).value
    if entry is None: 
        # could be subtotal row
        label = ws.cell(r,1).value
        if label and 'SUBTOTAL' in str(label):
            print('SUBTOTAL row:', [ws.cell(r,c).value for c in range(1,8)])
        continue
    date = ws.cell(r,2).value
    tk = ws.cell(r,3).value
    role = ws.cell(r,4).value
    rate = ws.cell(r,5).value
    hrs = ws.cell(r,6).value
    fee = ws.cell(r,7).value
    code = ws.cell(r,8).value
    desc = ws.cell(r,9).value
    if tk is None: 
        continue
    totals[tk][0]+=hrs
    totals[tk][1]+=fee
    entries.append((entry,date,tk,role,rate,hrs,fee,code,desc))
print('=== Per-timekeeper totals (computed) ===')
for tk,(h,f) in totals.items():
    print(f'{tk:20s} hrs={h:6.1f}  fee={f:11.2f}')
print('TOTAL hrs=', sum(v[0] for v in totals.values()), ' fee=', sum(v[1] for v in totals.values()))
print('Num entries:', len(entries))
""}

        'rate': ws.cell(r,5).value,

    }

# ---- Classifications ----
UNAUTH = {'Nina Barros','Priya Sengupta'}
# Section 4.3 full rejections (excess timekeepers)
R43 = {26, 62}
# Section 6.1 rate reductions (pure single-task inappropriate entries)
R61_JR = {43}      # partner cite-checking -> junior associate rate $375
R61_CA = {79}      # junior associate routine doc review -> contract attorney rate $185
ADMIN_WO = {15}    # administrative scheduling -> full writeoff

 # Hargrove

}

def classify(eid):

    if tk in UNAUTH:

    if eid in R43:

    if eid in ADMIN_WO:

    if eid in R61_JR:

# Compute reductions

detail_total_hrs = sum(e['hrs'] for e in entries.values())

    elif c=='admin':

    elif c=='r61_jr':
        pay = hrs*375; red = fee-pay; note='Partner cite-checking (Sec 6.1(b)) -> junior assoc rate $375'

"""Generate a .docx from markdown via Pandoc with an optional reference template.

Usage:
    python generate_from_md.py input.md output.docx [template.docx]

Pandoc applies the template's paragraph and run styles to your markdown.
Best for reports/memos/letters where the template carries firm styling.

Requires pandoc on PATH.
"""
import subprocess
import sys
from pathlib import Path


def generate(md_path: Path, output_path: Path, template_path: Path | None = None):
    output_path.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    cmd = ["pandoc", str(md_path), "-o", str(output_path)]
    if template_path is not None:
        cmd.append(f"--reference-doc={template_path}")
    result = subprocess.run(cmd, capture_output=True, text=True)
    if result.returncode != 0:
        print(f"pandoc failed: {result.stderr}", file=sys.stderr)
        sys.exit(1)
    print(f"OK: wrote {output_path}")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    if len(sys.argv) not in (3, 4):
        print("Usage: generate_from_md.py <input.md> <output.docx> [template.docx]", file=sys.stderr)
        sys.exit(2)
    template = Path(sys.argv[3]) if len(sys.argv) == 4 else None
    generate(Path(sys.argv[1]), Path(sys.argv[2]), template)

{"file_path": "memo.md", "content": "# INVOICE REVIEW MEMORANDUM

**PRIVILEGED & CONFIDENTIAL — ATTORNEY WORK PRODUCT / PREPARED IN ANTICIPATION OF LITIGATION**

| | |
|---|---|
| **To:** | Rachel Dominguez, Associate General Counsel (Designated In-House Counsel); Sandra Whitmore, General Counsel |

| **Date:** | June 12, 2025 |
| **Re:** | Review of Invoice No. HL-2025-05-PIN — Hargrove & Linden LLP — Billing Period May 1–31, 2025 |
| **Matter:** | *Pinnacle Health Systems, Inc. v. MedCore Analytics, LLC*, Case No. 1:24-cv-04187-RWS (N.D. Ga.) |

---

## Executive Summary

Hargrove & Linden LLP ("H&L" or the "Firm") submitted Invoice No. HL-2025-05-PIN, dated June 5, 2025, for legal services rendered during May 2025. The invoice **requests payment of $201,822.50**, consisting of $193,372.50 in attorney fees and $8,450.00 in expenses (per the Summary page).

This review identifies **multiple material billing-guideline violations** and one **critical internal reconciliation defect**. The invoice **cannot be paid as submitted**. After applying the reductions required by Pinnacle's Outside Counsel Billing Guidelines (January 2023) (the "Billing Guidelines") and the Approved Discovery-Phase Staffing Plan (Feb. 10, 2025), the **recommended payable amount is $148,993.12** — a reduction of **$52,829.38 (26.2%)** from the amount requested.

The principal findings are:

1. **Internal reconciliation defect (§9.1).** The supporting Time Detail contains 117 line items totaling **$215,680.00** in attorney fees, which is **$22,307.50 more** than the $193,372.50 stated on the Summary page and in the Time Detail's own SUBTOTAL row. The per-timekeeper figures on the Summary likewise do not foot to the line items. The Firm must submit a corrected, reconciled invoice.

2. **Two unauthorized timekeepers (§4.2) — $33,400.00.** Nina Barros (mid-level associate, $23,275.00) and Priya Sengupta (junior associate, $10,125.00) billed the matter without the prior written approval required by §4.2. The April 28, 2025 staffing request for Ms. Barros was expressly *not* approved (Ms. Dominguez responded on May 1 that she would review and "circle back"), and no request was ever made for Ms. Sengupta, who is an impermissible *second* concurrent junior associate.

3. **Contract attorney over-rate (§5.1, §4.4) — $3,862.50.** Marcus Tate was billed at $210/hr, exceeding both the $185/hr pass-through rate approved in the Staffing Plan and the $200/hr §5.1 cap.

4. **Excess timekeepers at a meeting and a deposition (§4.3) — $3,312.50.** Five timekeepers billed a single May 8 internal strategy call (cap: 3) and three timekeepers billed the May 19 Liu deposition without the advance approval required for more than two (cap: 2 absent pre-approval).

5. **Pervasive block billing (§5.4) — $29,864.38.** Fifty-two entries combine two or more discrete tasks; the mandatory 25% reduction applies.

6. **Task-to-timekeeper and administrative-task violations (§6.1) — $2,597.50.** A partner performed cite-checking; a junior associate performed routine first-level document review; and a mid-level associate billed scheduling/logistics as attorney time.

7. **Budget overage without pre-approval (§7.3) and missing budget-to-actual reconciliation (§7.2, §9.1(g)).** The fees as submitted exceed the §7.3 ceiling ($189,750) without the required written pre-approval, and the invoice omits the budget-to-actual reconciliation required by §7.2/§9.1(g).

8. **Non-reimbursable travel expense (§6.4, §8.2, §8.3) — $2,100.00.** The deposition was held at H&L's own Atlanta office, so the "travel" (mileage, parking, meals) for two Atlanta-based H&L attorneys is local travel and non-reimbursable overhead; the $2,100 aggregate also exceeded the $1,000 pre-approval threshold.

The detailed analysis and reduction calculations follow.

---

## I. Documents Reviewed

| Document | Source |
|---|---|
| Invoice No. HL-2025-05-PIN (Summary, Time Detail, Expense Detail, Rate Schedule) | H&L, dated June 5, 2025 |
| Approved Discovery-Phase Staffing Plan | H&L letter to R. Dominguez, Feb. 10, 2025 (acknowledged/approved) |
| Pinnacle Outside Counsel Billing Guidelines (Jan. 1, 2023) | Pinnacle Office of the General Counsel |
| Staffing correspondence re: Nina Barros | Email thread, K. Cho / R. Dominguez, Apr. 28 – May 1, 2025 |
| Monthly Case Status Report — May 2025 | H&L letter to R. Dominguez, June 1, 2025 |

---

## II. Invoice Overview and Critical Reconciliation Defect

### A. Invoice as submitted (Summary page)

| Component | Amount |
|---|---|
| Attorney fees (subtotal) | $193,372.50 |
| Expenses (subtotal) | $8,450.00 |
| **Total due** | **$201,822.50** |
| Stated total attorney hours | 422.5 |

### B. The Time Detail does not foot to the Summary — material §9.1 defect

The Time Detail worksheet contains **117 individual time entries**. When those entries are summed, they total **483.5 hours and $215,680.00 in attorney fees** — **61.0 hours and $22,307.50 more** than the 422.5 hours / $193,372.50 reported on the Summary page and in the Time Detail's own SUBTOTAL row. The per-timekeeper totals on the Summary are likewise inconsistent with the line items:

| Timekeeper | Summary (stated) | Time Detail (actual) | Variance |
|---|---|---|---|
| David Hargrove | 38.5 hrs / $37,922.50 | 38.5 hrs / $37,922.50 | $0.00 |
| Karen Cho | 74.0 hrs / $46,250.00 | 89.5 hrs / $55,937.50 | +$9,687.50 |
| James Pettersson | 82.5 hrs / $39,187.50 | 91.0 hrs / $43,225.00 | +$4,037.50 |
| Nina Barros | 45.0 hrs / $21,375.00 | 49.0 hrs / $23,275.00 | +$1,900.00 |
| Tyler Webb | 35.0 hrs / $13,125.00 | 34.0 hrs / $12,750.00 | −$375.00 |
| Priya Sengupta | 27.5 hrs / $10,312.50 | 27.0 hrs / $10,125.00 | −$187.50 |
| Marcus Tate | 120.0 hrs / $25,200.00 | 154.5 hrs / $32,445.00 | +$7,245.00 |
| **Total** | **422.5 / $193,372.50** | **483.5 / $215,680.00** | **+$22,307.50** |

**Assessment.** An invoice whose supporting detail does not reconcile to its summary cannot be reliably audited or paid. This violates §9.1 (invoice must include an accurate summary and time detail) and §7.2 (budget-to-actual reporting). **The Firm must submit a corrected invoice in which the line-item detail foots to the summary and the per-timekeeper totals.** The line-item analysis in Part III is performed against the 117 entries in the Time Detail, which is the operative line-item record. Because the recommended payable amount ($148,993.12) is below *both* the Summary total ($201,822.50) and the Detail total ($224,130.00), it represents a reduction under either measure.

---

## III. Findings and Recommended Reductions

### A. Unauthorized Timekeepers — §4.2 — $33,400.00 (full rejection)

The Approved Staffing Plan is the "complete and exclusive" roster of authorized timekeepers. §4.2 requires prior *written* approval before any new timekeeper performs billable work, and provides that after-the-fact additions "are subject to reduction or rejection of all fees attributable to the unapproved timekeeper, at Pinnacle's sole discretion." Two timekeepers on this invoice were not approved.

**1. Nina Barros — 49.0 hours / $23,275.00 — full rejection.**

The Staffing Plan expressly states that "James Pettersson is the only mid-level associate assigned to this matter. No other mid-level associate is included on the approved staffing roster." On April 28, 2025, Karen Cho emailed Ms. Dominguez requesting that Ms. Barros be added. Ms. Dominguez replied on May 1, 2025: *"Before we can move forward on adding Nina Barros, though, let me review this with Sandra and get back to you… I'll try to circle back with you early next week."* **No approval was ever given.** The Firm's own Rate Schedule annotates Ms. Barros as "Pending Approval — Added May 2025," and the June 1 Case Status Report concedes her involvement was "consistent with the request submitted" — a request is not an approval. No exigent-circumstance notification was provided within 24 hours as §4.2 permits. All $23,275.00 is rejected.

**2. Priya Sengupta — 27.0 hours / $10,125.00 — full rejection.**

The Staffing Plan authorizes only **one** junior associate at a time (the "Rotating Pool"), with Tyler Webb as the current assignment, and states that "any proposal to assign more than one junior associate concurrently would constitute a staffing change requiring prior written approval under §4.2." The invoice bills **two** junior associates concurrently (Webb and Sengupta) throughout May. No staffing-change request for Ms. Sengupta appears anywhere in the record; the Rate Schedule merely notes "Added May 2025." Because Tyler Webb is the approved junior associate, Ms. Sengupta is the impermissible second junior associate, and all $10,125.00 is rejected.

> **Subtotal — §4.2: $33,400.00.** (Note: rejecting these timekeepers also moots the block-billing, task-level, and meeting-cap issues that would otherwise apply to their entries.)

### B. Contract Attorney Rate Exceeds Approved Rate and Cap — §5.1, §4.4 — $3,862.50

Marcus Tate (contract attorney via Redwood Document Solutions) was billed at **$210/hr**. The Staffing Plan approved a **$185/hr** pass-through rate (no markup), and §5.1 caps contract attorneys at **$200/hr**. The billed rate exceeds both. Per §5.1, rates above the cap "will be automatically reduced to the applicable cap"; the approved pass-through rate of $185/hr controls as the lower approved rate. The rate is reduced to $185/hr across all 154.5 hours billed.

> **Reduction: 154.5 hrs × ($210 − $185) = $3,862.50.**

### C. Excess Timekeepers — Internal Meeting and Deposition — §4.3 — $3,312.50

§4.3 caps billing timekeepers for any single internal meeting or conference call at **three (3)**, even for Substantive Case-Strategy Sessions, and caps deposition billing at **two (2)** absent advance approval requested no later than five business days before the deposition. No advance approval was requested for either event below.

**1. May 8 team strategy conference call — five timekeepers billed.** Entries 22 (Hargrove), 23 (Cho), 24 (Pettersson), 25 (Barros), and 26 (Webb) all bill the same internal call. After removing the unauthorized Ms. Barros, four approved timekeepers remain — one more than the three permitted. The most junior attendee, Tyler Webb (2.5 hrs / $937.50), is reduced in full as the excess timekeeper.

**2. May 19 deposition of Dr. Rebecca Liu — three timekeepers billed.** Entries 60 (Hargrove), 61 (Cho), and 62 (Pettersson) bill the deposition. Two attorneys (the lead partner observing/strategizing and the senior associate who conducted the direct examination) are reasonable without approval; the **third** timekeeper requires advance approval, which was not requested. James Pettersson (5.0 hrs / $2,375.00), who managed exhibits and took notes, is reduced in full as the excess timekeeper. (His exhibit/summary work could have been billed separately and at a more appropriate level, but was impermissibly block-billed with attendance — see §D.)

> **Subtotal — §4.3: $937.50 + $2,375.00 = $3,312.50.**

### D. Block Billing — §5.4 — $29,864.38

§5.4 prohibits combining two or more discrete tasks in a single line item and mandates an **automatic 25% reduction** to the fees of each block-billed entry. Fifty-two entries on this invoice combine distinct activity types (e.g., drafting + research; review/revise + client conference; attend deposition + post-deposition summary + team debrief; finalize brief + prepare order + coordinate filing). Several mirror the Guidelines' own examples almost verbatim (e.g., Entry 61: "Prepare for and attend deposition of Dr. Rebecca Liu …; post-deposition analysis and team debrief"; Entry 92: "Prepare for and conduct strategy session …; draft outline for expert discovery plan. Review and edit interrogatory responses …").

The 25% reduction is applied to each block-billed entry among the approved timekeepers (entries already fully rejected under §4.2 or §4.3 are excluded to avoid double-counting). The 52 affected entries aggregate $119,457.50 in fees.

> **Reduction: $119,457.50 × 25% = $29,864.38.** A complete entry-by-entry schedule appears in Appendix A.

### E. Task-to-Timekeeper Appropriateness — §6.1 — $1,885.00

§6.1 requires each task to be staffed to the lowest-cost competent timekeeper and specifies rate reductions where a senior timekeeper performs junior-level work.

**1. Partner cite-checking — Entry 43 — $1,220.00.** David Hargrove (partner, $985/hr) billed 2.0 hours to "Review and revise cite-check memorandum prepared by T. Webb; finalize citations for motion to compel brief." Cite-checking and citation verification are presumptively inappropriate for partners under §6.1(b) (appropriate timekeeper: junior associate or paralegal). The entry is reduced to the junior-associate rate of $375/hr. Reduction: 2.0 × ($985 − $375) = **$1,220.00**.

**2. Junior associate performing routine first-level document review — Entry 79 — $665.00.** Tyler Webb (junior associate, $375/hr) billed 3.5 hours to "Review third batch of MedCore production documents for relevance and privilege" — routine first-level review that §6.1(a) assigns to the contract attorney. The Case Status Report confirms Mr. Webb "assisted with document review on days of elevated volume." The entry is reduced to the contract-attorney rate of $185/hr. Reduction: 3.5 × ($375 − $185) = **$665.00**. (The narrative is also thin — no Bates range or coding detail — though the §6.1 reduction is the operative remedy.)

> **Subtotal — §6.1 task-level: $1,220.00 + $665.00 = $1,885.00.**

*Additional §6.1 flags (noted, addressed via block-billing reduction where applicable).* Entry 9 (Hargrove, 4.5 hrs) combines partner-level document review with preparation of a client memorandum; the document-review component is presumptively inappropriate for a partner under §6.1(a) and should, on resubmission, be billed at the appropriate lower rate. Several block-billed entries (e.g., 51, 77, 94, 113, 114) embed administrative "coordinate logistics / filing / scheduling" or billing-summary components; because those entries are block-billed, the 25% reduction is applied and the administrative portion is flagged for separation on resubmission. Entry 56 (Pettersson) reflects a mid-level associate preparing deposition exhibit binders — work §6.1(c) assigns to a paralegal or junior associate; while §6.1's mandatory rate reduction targets partners/senior associates, this over-leveling is an efficiency concern to monitor.

### F. Administrative Tasks Billed at Attorney Rates — §6.1(g)–(k), §2 — $712.50

Entry 15 (Pettersson, 1.5 hrs / $712.50): "Schedule depositions of three remaining witnesses; coordinate court reporter availability; confirm deposition locations." Scheduling depositions and coordinating court reporters are Administrative Tasks that §2 defines as non-compensable overhead and that §6.1(g)–(h) renders presumptively inappropriate for *any* attorney. The entry is written off in full.

> **Subtotal — administrative: $712.50.**

### G. Budget Overage Without Pre-Approval; Missing Reconciliation — §7.2, §7.3, §9.1

**§7.3 overage.** The Approved Staffing Plan set a monthly fee range of **$95,000–$165,000**, so the maximum payable without pre-approval is 115% of the high end, or **$189,750**. The fees as submitted ($193,372.50 per the Summary; $215,680.00 per the Detail) **exceed that ceiling without the written pre-approval §7.3 requires**. No pre-approval was requested or given. This is a compliance violation. Because the guideline-compliance reductions in Parts A–F bring the compliant fees to $142,643.12 — *below* the $189,750 ceiling — no *additional* dollar reduction under §7.3 is necessary; the specific reductions are more stringent than the cap. The §7.3 violation is nonetheless documented and should be addressed with the Firm.

**§7.2 / §9.1(g) — missing budget-to-actual reconciliation.** The invoice contains no budget-to-actual reconciliation (approved range, actual fees/expenses, cumulative figures, percentage of phase budget consumed, and narrative of variances >10%). This is a required invoice component. The Firm must supply it with the corrected invoice.

**§9.1(a) — matter number.** The invoice identifies the matter by name but does not state a Pinnacle matter number. Minor; to be corrected on resubmission.

### H. Expense Review — §8 — $2,100.00 disallowed

| # | Category | Vendor | Amount | Disposition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Court reporting | Grayson Court Reporting Services | $3,200.00 | **Allowed, with flag** — see below |
| 2 | Travel | Various | $2,100.00 | **Disallowed in full** |
| 3 | E-discovery hosting | Tripoint Litigation Support LLC | $2,650.00 | Allowed (retain pre-approval documentation) |
| 4 | Courier / delivery | Various | $500.00 | Allowed |

**Expense 2 — Travel ($2,100.00) disallowed.** The Case Status Report states the Liu deposition was taken "at the offices of Hargrove & Linden LLP in Atlanta, Georgia." The "travel" for two Atlanta-based H&L attorneys to their own firm's office is **local travel within the Atlanta metropolitan area**, which §6.4 renders "not billable under any circumstances." The meals component is likewise non-reimbursable: §8.2 allows meals only during approved out-of-town travel or working sessions past 8:00 p.m., neither of which is indicated. Independently, the $2,100 aggregate exceeds the $1,000 pre-approval threshold of §8.3, and no pre-approval was obtained. The full $2,100.00 is disallowed.

**Expense 1 — Court reporting ($3,200.00) — flag, pending documentation.** Grayson is an approved vendor (Appendix B) and court-reporter/transcript fees are reimbursable under §8.1, but §8.1 limits transcript reimbursement to "one copy per deposition, at standard rates." The description references "expedited delivery," a non-standard premium for which no pre-approval was obtained. The Firm should furnish an itemized vendor invoice separating the standard original-plus-one-copy charges from the expedited premium; any expedited premium is recommended for disallowance absent pre-approval. No dollar reduction is taken in this memo pending that breakdown.

**Expense 3 — E-discovery ($2,650.00) — allowed.** Tripoint is the approved e-discovery vendor and the monthly hosting charge is reasonable for an ~85,000-document production. §8.1 makes e-discovery charges "subject to pre-approval"; the Firm should retain the pre-approval documentation and confirm it for future invoices.

**Expense 4 — Courier ($500.00) — allowed.** Messenger/courier services for time-sensitive service and filing are reimbursable under §8.1. (Note: to the extent any portion relates to electronic filing that did not require courier service, it should be separated on future invoices.)

> **Expense reduction: $2,100.00. Compliant expenses: $6,350.00** (subject to the court-reporting adjustment once itemized).

### I. Items Reviewed and Found Compliant

- **Rate caps (§5.1).** The approved timekeepers' rates are within caps: Hargrove $985 (cap $1,000); Cho $625 (cap $650); Pettersson $475 (cap $500); Webb $375 (cap $400). Only Tate's $210 rate exceeded the cap/approved rate (addressed in Part B).
- **Billing increments (§5.5).** All entries are recorded in 0.5-hour (or smaller 0.1-multiple) increments; no impermissible quarter-hour increments were found.
- **Time-entry narratives (§9.2).** With the exception of Entry 79 (noted in Part E), narratives generally identify the specific subject, document, or issue and satisfy §9.2.
- **Approved vendors (§8.4, Appendix B).** E-discovery (Tripoint), court reporting (Grayson), and contract-attorney staffing (Redwood) all used approved vendors.
- **Staffing-plan hour variances (informational).** Mr. Hargrove's 38.5 hours exceed his 15–25 hr/month estimate (the Case Status Report explains this was driven by the Liu deposition, the in-house strategy session, and personal document review, and projects a return to normal levels). Ms. Cho (89.5), Mr. Pettersson (91.0), and Mr. Tate (154.5) modestly exceed their plan ranges on the Detail. These are variances to monitor, not independent bases for reduction; the binding constraint is the §7.3 budget ceiling, addressed in Part G.

## VI. Recommendations and Next Steps

1. **Do not pay the invoice as submitted.** Return Invoice No. HL-2025-05-PIN with the reductions identified above. Provisional recommended payable: **$148,993.12**, contingent on resolution of the items below.

2. **Require a corrected, reconciled invoice (§9.1, §7.2).** The Firm must resubmit with (a) a Time Detail that foots to the Summary and to the per-timekeeper totals; (b) the omitted budget-to-actual reconciliation; and (c) the Pinnacle matter number. The $22,307.50 discrepancy must be explained.

3. **Reject all fees of Nina Barros and Priya Sengupta (§4.2).** If the Firm wishes to add either timekeeper prospectively, it must submit a written §4.2 staffing-change request (name, role, rate, reason, anticipated hours, and budget impact) and obtain written approval *before* further work. Note that adding Ms. Barros would require amending the "single mid-level associate" term of the Staffing Plan, and adding Ms. Sengupta would require amending the "one junior associate at a time" term.

4. **Adjust the contract-attorney rate to $185/hr** prospectively and confirm the Redwood pass-through rate in writing.

5. **Require advance approval for future depositions exceeding two billing timekeepers** (§4.3), requested no later than five business days in advance, and limit internal meetings to three billing timekeepers.

6. **Require compliance with §5.4 (no block billing).** The Firm may, within 15 days of notice, resubmit the block-billed entries as separate, individually timed line items to avoid the 25% reduction; resubmitted entries must reasonably account for the total time originally reported and must reflect appropriate task-to-timekeeper staffing.

7. **Address the §7.3 budget overage.** Obtain written pre-approval before any future month is expected to exceed $189,750, and require the Firm to manage staffing to the approved range.

8. **Disallow the $2,100 travel expense** and require an itemized court-reporting invoice (Expense 1) to substantiate the standard-rate portion and isolate any expedited premium.

9. **Resubmission deadline.** Request the corrected invoice and any §5.4/§9.2 resubmissions within 15 days, consistent with the Guidelines' cure periods.

{"file_path": "memo.md", "content": "# INVOICE REVIEW MEMORANDUM

**PRIVILEGED & CONFIDENTIAL — ATTORNEY WORK PRODUCT / PREPARED IN ANTICIPATION OF LITIGATION**

| | |
|---|---|
| **To:** | Rachel Dominguez, Associate General Counsel (Designated In-House Counsel); Sandra Whitmore, General Counsel |
| **From:** | Legal Department — Invoice Review, Pinnacle Health Systems, Inc. |
| **Date:** | June 12, 2025 |
| **Re:** | Review of Invoice No. HL-2025-05-PIN — Hargrove & Linden LLP — Billing Period May 1–31, 2025 |
| **Matter:** | *Pinnacle Health Systems, Inc. v. MedCore Analytics, LLC*, Case No. 1:24-cv-04187-RWS (N.D. Ga.) |

---

## Executive Summary

Hargrove & Linden LLP ("H&L" or the "Firm") submitted Invoice No. HL-2025-05-PIN, dated June 5, 2025, for legal services rendered during May 2025. The invoice **requests payment of $201,822.50**, consisting of $193,372.50 in attorney fees and $8,450.00 in expenses (per the Summary page).

This review identifies **multiple material billing-guideline violations** and one **critical internal reconciliation defect**. The invoice **cannot be paid as submitted**. After applying the reductions required by Pinnacle's Outside Counsel Billing Guidelines (January 2023) (the "Billing Guidelines") and the Approved Discovery-Phase Staffing Plan (Feb. 10, 2025), the **recommended payable amount is $148,993.12** — a reduction of **$52,829.38 (26.2%)** from the amount requested.

The principal findings are:

1. **Internal reconciliation defect (§9.1).** The supporting Time Detail contains 117 line items totaling **$215,680.00** in attorney fees, which is **$22,307.50 more** than the $193,372.50 stated on the Summary page and in the Time Detail's own SUBTOTAL row. The per-timekeeper figures on the Summary likewise do not foot to the line items. The Firm must submit a corrected, reconciled invoice.

2. **Two unauthorized timekeepers (§4.2) — $33,400.00.** Nina Barros (mid-level associate, $23,275.00) and Priya Sengupta (junior associate, $10,125.00) billed the matter without the prior written approval required by §4.2. The April 28, 2025 staffing request for Ms. Barros was expressly *not* approved (Ms. Dominguez responded on May 1 that she would review and "circle back"), and no request was ever made for Ms. Sengupta, who is an impermissible *second* concurrent junior associate.

3. **Contract attorney over-rate (§5.1, §4.4) — $3,862.50.** Marcus Tate was billed at $210/hr, exceeding both the $185/hr pass-through rate approved in the Staffing Plan and the $200/hr §5.1 cap.

4. **Excess timekeepers at a meeting and a deposition (§4.3) — $3,312.50.** Five timekeepers billed a single May 8 internal strategy call (cap: 3) and three timekeepers billed the May 19 Liu deposition without the advance approval required for more than two (cap: 2 absent pre-approval).

5. **Pervasive block billing (§5.4) — $29,864.38.** Fifty-two entries combine two or more discrete tasks; the mandatory 25% reduction applies.

6. **Task-to-timekeeper and administrative-task violations (§6.1) — $2,597.50.** A partner performed cite-checking; a junior associate performed routine first-level document review; and a mid-level associate billed scheduling/logistics as attorney time.

7. **Budget overage without pre-approval (§7.3) and missing budget-to-actual reconciliation (§7.2, §9.1(g)).** The fees as submitted exceed the §7.3 ceiling ($189,750) without the required written pre-approval, and the invoice omits the budget-to-actual reconciliation required by §7.2/§9.1(g).

8. **Non-reimbursable travel expense (§6.4, §8.2, §8.3) — $2,100.00.** The deposition was held at H&L's own Atlanta office, so the "travel" (mileage, parking, meals) for two Atlanta-based H&L attorneys is local travel and non-reimbursable overhead; the $2,100 aggregate also exceeded the $1,000 pre-approval threshold.

The detailed analysis and reduction calculations follow.

---

## I. Documents Reviewed

| Document | Source |
|---|---|
| Invoice No. HL-2025-05-PIN (Summary, Time Detail, Expense Detail, Rate Schedule) | H&L, dated June 5, 2025 |
| Approved Discovery-Phase Staffing Plan | H&L letter to R. Dominguez, Feb. 10, 2025 (acknowledged/approved) |
| Pinnacle Outside Counsel Billing Guidelines (Jan. 1, 2023) | Pinnacle Office of the General Counsel |
| Staffing correspondence re: Nina Barros | Email thread, K. Cho / R. Dominguez, Apr. 28 – May 1, 2025 |
| Monthly Case Status Report — May 2025 | H&L letter to R. Dominguez, June 1, 2025 |

---

## II. Invoice Overview and Critical Reconciliation Defect

### A. Invoice as submitted (Summary page)

| Component | Amount |
|---|---|
| Attorney fees (subtotal) | $193,372.50 |
| Expenses (subtotal) | $8,450.00 |
| **Total due** | **$201,822.50** |
| Stated total attorney hours | 422.5 |

### B. The Time Detail does not foot to the Summary — material §9.1 defect

The Time Detail worksheet contains **117 individual time entries**. When those entries are summed, they total **483.5 hours and $215,680.00 in attorney fees** — **61.0 hours and $22,307.50 more** than the 422.5 hours / $193,372.50 reported on the Summary page and in the Time Detail's own SUBTOTAL row. The per-timekeeper totals on the Summary are likewise inconsistent with the line items:

| Timekeeper | Summary (stated) | Time Detail (actual) | Variance |
|---|---|---|---|
| David Hargrove | 38.5 hrs / $37,922.50 | 38.5 hrs / $37,922.50 | $0.00 |
| Karen Cho | 74.0 hrs / $46,250.00 | 89.5 hrs / $55,937.50 | +$9,687.50 |
| James Pettersson | 82.5 hrs / $39,187.50 | 91.0 hrs / $43,225.00 | +$4,037.50 |
| Nina Barros | 45.0 hrs / $21,375.00 | 49.0 hrs / $23,275.00 | +$1,900.00 |
| Tyler Webb | 35.0 hrs / $13,125.00 | 34.0 hrs / $12,750.00 | −$375.00 |
| Priya Sengupta | 27.5 hrs / $10,312.50 | 27.0 hrs / $10,125.00 | −$187.50 |
| Marcus Tate | 120.0 hrs / $25,200.00 | 154.5 hrs / $32,445.00 | +$7,245.00 |
| **Total** | **422.5 / $193,372.50** | **483.5 / $215,680.00** | **+$22,307.50** |

**Assessment.** An invoice whose supporting detail does not reconcile to its summary cannot be reliably audited or paid. This violates §9.1 (invoice must include an accurate summary and time detail) and §7.2 (budget-to-actual reporting). **The Firm must submit a corrected invoice in which the line-item detail foots to the summary and the per-timekeeper totals.** The line-item analysis in Part III is performed against the 117 entries in the Time Detail, which is the operative line-item record. Because the recommended payable amount ($148,993.12) is below *both* the Summary total ($201,822.50) and the Detail total ($224,130.00), it represents a reduction under either measure.

---

## III. Findings and Recommended Reductions

### A. Unauthorized Timekeepers — §4.2 — $33,400.00 (full rejection)

The Approved Staffing Plan is the "complete and exclusive" roster of authorized timekeepers. §4.2 requires prior *written* approval before any new timekeeper performs billable work, and provides that after-the-fact additions "are subject to reduction or rejection of all fees attributable to the unapproved timekeeper, at Pinnacle's sole discretion." Two timekeepers on this invoice were not approved.

**1. Nina Barros — 49.0 hours / $23,275.00 — full rejection.**

The Staffing Plan expressly states that "James Pettersson is the only mid-level associate assigned to this matter. No other mid-level associate is included on the approved staffing roster." On April 28, 2025, Karen Cho emailed Ms. Dominguez requesting that Ms. Barros be added. Ms. Dominguez replied on May 1, 2025: *"Before we can move forward on adding Nina Barros, though, let me review this with Sandra and get back to you… I'll try to circle back with you early next week."* **No approval was ever given.** The Firm's own Rate Schedule annotates Ms. Barros as "Pending Approval — Added May 2025," and the June 1 Case Status Report concedes her involvement was "consistent with the request submitted" — a request is not an approval. No exigent-circumstance notification was provided within 24 hours as §4.2 permits. All $23,275.00 is rejected.

**2. Priya Sengupta — 27.0 hours / $10,125.00 — full rejection.**

The Staffing Plan authorizes only **one** junior associate at a time (the "Rotating Pool"), with Tyler Webb as the current assignment, and states that "any proposal to assign more than one junior associate concurrently would constitute a staffing change requiring prior written approval under §4.2." The invoice bills **two** junior associates concurrently (Webb and Sengupta) throughout May. No staffing-change request for Ms. Sengupta appears anywhere in the record; the Rate Schedule merely notes "Added May 2025." Because Tyler Webb is the approved junior associate, Ms. Sengupta is the impermissible second junior associate, and all $10,125.00 is rejected.

> **Subtotal — §4.2: $33,400.00.** (Note: rejecting these timekeepers also moots the block-billing, task-level, and meeting-cap issues that would otherwise apply to their entries.)

### B. Contract Attorney Rate Exceeds Approved Rate and Cap — §5.1, §4.4 — $3,862.50

Marcus Tate (contract attorney via Redwood Document Solutions) was billed at **$210/hr**. The Staffing Plan approved a **$185/hr** pass-through rate (no markup), and §5.1 caps contract attorneys at **$200/hr**. The billed rate exceeds both. Per §5.1, rates above the cap "will be automatically reduced to the applicable cap"; the approved pass-through rate of $185/hr controls as the lower approved rate. The rate is reduced to $185/hr across all 154.5 hours billed.

> **Reduction: 154.5 hrs × ($210 − $185) = $3,862.50.**

### C. Excess Timekeepers — Internal Meeting and Deposition — §4.3 — $3,312.50

§4.3 caps billing timekeepers for any single internal meeting or conference call at **three (3)**, even for Substantive Case-Strategy Sessions, and caps deposition billing at **two (2)** absent advance approval requested no later than five business days before the deposition. No advance approval was requested for either event below.

**1. May 8 team strategy conference call — five timekeepers billed.** Entries 22 (Hargrove), 23 (Cho), 24 (Pettersson), 25 (Barros), and 26 (Webb) all bill the same internal call. After removing the unauthorized Ms. Barros, four approved timekeepers remain — one more than the three permitted. The most junior attendee, Tyler Webb (2.5 hrs / $937.50), is reduced in full as the excess timekeeper.

**2. May 19 deposition of Dr. Rebecca Liu — three timekeepers billed.** Entries 60 (Hargrove), 61 (Cho), and 62 (Pettersson) bill the deposition. Two attorneys (the lead partner observing/strategizing and the senior associate who conducted the direct examination) are reasonable without approval; the **third** timekeeper requires advance approval, which was not requested. James Pettersson (5.0 hrs / $2,375.00), who managed exhibits and took notes, is reduced in full as the excess timekeeper. (His exhibit/summary work could have been billed separately and at a more appropriate level, but was impermissibly block-billed with attendance — see §D.)

> **Subtotal — §4.3: $937.50 + $2,375.00 = $3,312.50.**

### D. Block Billing — §5.4 — $29,864.38

§5.4 prohibits combining two or more discrete tasks in a single line item and mandates an **automatic 25% reduction** to the fees of each block-billed entry. Fifty-two entries on this invoice combine distinct activity types (e.g., drafting + research; review/revise + client conference; attend deposition + post-deposition summary + team debrief; finalize brief + prepare order + coordinate filing). Several mirror the Guidelines' own examples almost verbatim (e.g., Entry 61: "Prepare for and attend deposition of Dr. Rebecca Liu …; post-deposition analysis and team debrief"; Entry 92: "Prepare for and conduct strategy session …; draft outline for expert discovery plan. Review and edit interrogatory responses …").

The 25% reduction is applied to each block-billed entry among the approved timekeepers (entries already fully rejected under §4.2 or §4.3 are excluded to avoid double-counting). The 52 affected entries aggregate $119,457.50 in fees.

> **Reduction: $119,457.50 × 25% = $29,864.38.** A complete entry-by-entry schedule appears in Appendix A.

### E. Task-to-Timekeeper Appropriateness — §6.1 — $1,885.00

§6.1 requires each task to be staffed to the lowest-cost competent timekeeper and specifies rate reductions where a senior timekeeper performs junior-level work.

**1. Partner cite-checking — Entry 43 — $1,220.00.** David Hargrove (partner, $985/hr) billed 2.0 hours to "Review and revise cite-check memorandum prepared by T. Webb; finalize citations for motion to compel brief." Cite-checking and citation verification are presumptively inappropriate for partners under §6.1(b) (appropriate timekeeper: junior associate or paralegal). The entry is reduced to the junior-associate rate of $375/hr. Reduction: 2.0 × ($985 − $375) = **$1,220.00**.

**2. Junior associate performing routine first-level document review — Entry 79 — $665.00.** Tyler Webb (junior associate, $375/hr) billed 3.5 hours to "Review third batch of MedCore production documents for relevance and privilege" — routine first-level review that §6.1(a) assigns to the contract attorney. The Case Status Report confirms Mr. Webb "assisted with document review on days of elevated volume." The entry is reduced to the contract-attorney rate of $185/hr. Reduction: 3.5 × ($375 − $185) = **$665.00**. (The narrative is also thin — no Bates range or coding detail — though the §6.1 reduction is the operative remedy.)

> **Subtotal — §6.1 task-level: $1,220.00 + $665.00 = $1,885.00.**

*Additional §6.1 flags (noted, addressed via block-billing reduction where applicable).* Entry 9 (Hargrove, 4.5 hrs) combines partner-level document review with preparation of a client memorandum; the document-review component is presumptively inappropriate for a partner under §6.1(a) and should, on resubmission, be billed at the appropriate lower rate. Several block-billed entries (e.g., 51, 77, 94, 113, 114) embed administrative "coordinate logistics / filing / scheduling" or billing-summary components; because those entries are block-billed, the 25% reduction is applied and the administrative portion is flagged for separation on resubmission. Entry 56 (Pettersson) reflects a mid-level associate preparing deposition exhibit binders — work §6.1(c) assigns to a paralegal or junior associate; while §6.1's mandatory rate reduction targets partners/senior associates, this over-leveling is an efficiency concern to monitor.

### F. Administrative Tasks Billed at Attorney Rates — §6.1(g)–(k), §2 — $712.50

Entry 15 (Pettersson, 1.5 hrs / $712.50): "Schedule depositions of three remaining witnesses; coordinate court reporter availability; confirm deposition locations." Scheduling depositions and coordinating court reporters are Administrative Tasks that §2 defines as non-compensable overhead and that §6.1(g)–(h) renders presumptively inappropriate for *any* attorney. The entry is written off in full.

> **Subtotal — administrative: $712.50.**

### G. Budget Overage Without Pre-Approval; Missing Reconciliation — §7.2, §7.3, §9.1

**§7.3 overage.** The Approved Staffing Plan set a monthly fee range of **$95,000–$165,000**, so the maximum payable without pre-approval is 115% of the high end, or **$189,750**. The fees as submitted ($193,372.50 per the Summary; $215,680.00 per the Detail) **exceed that ceiling without the written pre-approval §7.3 requires**. No pre-approval was requested or given. This is a compliance violation. Because the guideline-compliance reductions in Parts A–F bring the compliant fees to $142,643.12 — *below* the $189,750 ceiling — no *additional* dollar reduction under §7.3 is necessary; the specific reductions are more stringent than the cap. The §7.3 violation is nonetheless documented and should be addressed with the Firm.

**§7.2 / §9.1(g) — missing budget-to-actual reconciliation.** The invoice contains no budget-to-actual reconciliation (approved range, actual fees/expenses, cumulative figures, percentage of phase budget consumed, and narrative of variances >10%). This is a required invoice component. The Firm must supply it with the corrected invoice.

**§9.1(a) — matter number.** The invoice identifies the matter by name but does not state a Pinnacle matter number. Minor; to be corrected on resubmission.

### H. Expense Review — §8 — $2,100.00 disallowed

| # | Category | Vendor | Amount | Disposition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Court reporting | Grayson Court Reporting Services | $3,200.00 | **Allowed, with flag** — see below |
| 2 | Travel | Various | $2,100.00 | **Disallowed in full** |
| 3 | E-discovery hosting | Tripoint Litigation Support LLC | $2,650.00 | Allowed (retain pre-approval documentation) |
| 4 | Courier / delivery | Various | $500.00 | Allowed |

**Expense 2 — Travel ($2,100.00) disallowed.** The Case Status Report states the Liu deposition was taken "at the offices of Hargrove & Linden LLP in Atlanta, Georgia." The "travel" for two Atlanta-based H&L attorneys to their own firm's office is **local travel within the Atlanta metropolitan area**, which §6.4 renders "not billable under any circumstances." The meals component is likewise non-reimbursable: §8.2 allows meals only during approved out-of-town travel or working sessions past 8:00 p.m., neither of which is indicated. Independently, the $2,100 aggregate exceeds the $1,000 pre-approval threshold of §8.3, and no pre-approval was obtained. The full $2,100.00 is disallowed.

**Expense 1 — Court reporting ($3,200.00) — flag, pending documentation.** Grayson is an approved vendor (Appendix B) and court-reporter/transcript fees are reimbursable under §8.1, but §8.1 limits transcript reimbursement to "one copy per deposition, at standard rates." The description references "expedited delivery," a non-standard premium for which no pre-approval was obtained. The Firm should furnish an itemized vendor invoice separating the standard original-plus-one-copy charges from the expedited premium; any expedited premium is recommended for disallowance absent pre-approval. No dollar reduction is taken in this memo pending that breakdown.

**Expense 3 — E-discovery ($2,650.00) — allowed.** Tripoint is the approved e-discovery vendor and the monthly hosting charge is reasonable for an ~85,000-document production. §8.1 makes e-discovery charges "subject to pre-approval"; the Firm should retain the pre-approval documentation and confirm it for future invoices.

**Expense 4 — Courier ($500.00) — allowed.** Messenger/courier services for time-sensitive service and filing are reimbursable under §8.1. (Note: to the extent any portion relates to electronic filing that did not require courier service, it should be separated on future invoices.)

> **Expense reduction: $2,100.00. Compliant expenses: $6,350.00** (subject to the court-reporting adjustment once itemized).

### I. Items Reviewed and Found Compliant

- **Rate caps (§5.1).** The approved timekeepers' rates are within caps: Hargrove $985 (cap $1,000); Cho $625 (cap $650); Pettersson $475 (cap $500); Webb $375 (cap $400). Only Tate's $210 rate exceeded the cap/approved rate (addressed in Part B).
- **Billing increments (§5.5).** All entries are recorded in 0.5-hour (or smaller 0.1-multiple) increments; no impermissible quarter-hour increments were found.
- **Time-entry narratives (§9.2).** With the exception of Entry 79 (noted in Part E), narratives generally identify the specific subject, document, or issue and satisfy §9.2.
- **Approved vendors (§8.4, Appendix B).** E-discovery (Tripoint), court reporting (Grayson), and contract-attorney staffing (Redwood) all used approved vendors.
- **Staffing-plan hour variances (informational).** Mr. Hargrove's 38.5 hours exceed his 15–25 hr/month estimate (the Case Status Report explains this was driven by the Liu deposition, the in-house strategy session, and personal document review, and projects a return to normal levels). Ms. Cho (89.5), Mr. Pettersson (91.0), and Mr. Tate (154.5) modestly exceed their plan ranges on the Detail. These are variances to monitor, not independent bases for reduction; the binding constraint is the §7.3 budget ceiling, addressed in Part G.

## VI. Recommendations and Next Steps

1. **Do not pay the invoice as submitted.** Return Invoice No. HL-2025-05-PIN with the reductions identified above. Provisional recommended payable: **$148,993.12**, contingent on resolution of the items below.

2. **Require a corrected, reconciled invoice (§9.1, §7.2).** The Firm must resubmit with (a) a Time Detail that foots to the Summary and to the per-timekeeper totals; (b) the omitted budget-to-actual reconciliation; and (c) the Pinnacle matter number. The $22,307.50 discrepancy must be explained.

3. **Reject all fees of Nina Barros and Priya Sengupta (§4.2).** If the Firm wishes to add either timekeeper prospectively, it must submit a written §4.2 staffing-change request (name, role, rate, reason, anticipated hours, and budget impact) and obtain written approval *before* further work. Note that adding Ms. Barros would require amending the "single mid-level associate" term of the Staffing Plan, and adding Ms. Sengupta would require amending the "one junior associate at a time" term.

4. **Adjust the contract-attorney rate to $185/hr** prospectively and confirm the Redwood pass-through rate in writing.

5. **Require advance approval for future depositions exceeding two billing timekeepers** (§4.3), requested no later than five business days in advance, and limit internal meetings to three billing timekeepers.

6. **Require compliance with §5.4 (no block billing).** The Firm may, within 15 days of notice, resubmit the block-billed entries as separate, individually timed line items to avoid the 25% reduction; resubmitted entries must reasonably account for the total time originally reported and must reflect appropriate task-to-timekeeper staffing.

7. **Address the §7.3 budget overage.** Obtain written pre-approval before any future month is expected to exceed $189,750, and require the Firm to manage staffing to the approved range.

8. **Disallow the $2,100 travel expense** and require an itemized court-reporting invoice (Expense 1) to substantiate the standard-rate portion and isolate any expedited premium.

9. **Resubmission deadline.** Request the corrected invoice and any §5.4/§9.2 resubmissions within 15 days, consistent with the Guidelines' cure periods.

The deliverable is complete and validated. Here is a summary of what I produced.

## Deliverable: `output/invoice-review-memo.docx`

A detailed, schema-valid Word memorandum reviewing Hargrove & Linden LLP Invoice No. HL-2025-05-PIN against the Approved Staffing Plan, Pinnacle's Billing Guidelines, the staffing emails, and the May 2025 Case Status Report.

### Key findings and reduction calculations

**Critical defect — internal reconciliation (§9.1):** The 117 line items in the Time Detail total **$215,680.00** in fees, but the Summary page (and the detail's own SUBTOTAL row) claim only **$193,372.50** — a **$22,307.50** discrepancy. The per-timekeeper totals don't foot either. The invoice cannot be paid as submitted; a corrected, reconciled invoice is required.

**Attorney-fee reductions ($73,036.88):**

| Ref. | Issue | Guideline | Reduction |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | Unauthorized timekeepers — Nina Barros ($23,275) + Priya Sengupta ($10,125) | §4.2 | $33,400.00 |
| B | Contract attorney billed at $210 vs. approved $185 (and $200 cap) | §5.1, §4.4 | $3,862.50 |
| C | Excess timekeepers — May 8 call (5 TKs, cap 3) + May 19 deposition (3 TKs, cap 2) | §4.3 | $3,312.50 |
| D | Block billing — 52 entries × 25% | §5.4 | $29,864.38 |
| E | Partner cite-checking + junior-associate routine doc review | §6.1 | $1,885.00 |
| F | Administrative scheduling billed as attorney time | §6.1(g)–(h) | $712.50 |

**Expense reduction:** $2,100.00 travel disallowed (deposition was at H&L's own Atlanta office → local travel; meals not out-of-town; no >$1,000 pre-approval). Court-reporting expedited premium flagged pending an itemized breakdown.

**Budget (§7.3):** Fees as submitted exceed the $189,750 ceiling without pre-approval — a documented violation; the specific reductions bring compliant fees below the ceiling, so no additional cap reduction is needed. Missing budget-to-actual reconciliation (§7.2/§9.1(g)) also flagged.

### Bottom line

| | Amount |
|---|---|
| Compliant attorney fees | $142,643.12 |
| Compliant expenses | $6,350.00 |
| **Recommended total payable** | **$148,993.12** |
| Amount requested | $201,822.50 |
| **Total reduction** | **$52,829.38 (26.2%)** |

The memo includes an executive summary, document-by-document findings tied to specific guideline sections, a reduction-calculation summary, a per-timekeeper reconciliation, recommendations/next steps, and two appendices (a full 52-entry block-billing schedule and a fully-rejected/rate-reduced entry schedule).
